## Random forests make trees less fragile

A single decision tree is easy to explain and easy to overfit. Random forests keep
the tree logic but reduce that fragility by averaging many trees instead of trusting
one unstable partition of the data.

That is why random forests matter in `TEM 0052`: they are often the first genuinely
strong tabular-data benchmark that outperforms a single tree without requiring the
full complexity of boosting.

## Two sources of randomness

Random forests combine two ideas:

1. bootstrap aggregation, where each tree is fit on a resampled version of the
   training data,
2. random feature selection, where each split only considers a subset of the
   available predictors.

The first step makes the individual trees see slightly different data. The second
step reduces the chance that every tree keeps choosing the same dominant predictors.

The result is a collection of de-correlated trees whose predictions are aggregated by
majority vote in classification or averaging in regression.

## Why forests usually beat a single tree

The core gain from a random forest is variance reduction.

- a single deep tree can react strongly to noise,
- averaging many trees smooths out those unstable local decisions,
- and feature randomness makes the ensemble less dependent on one lucky split path.

This does not make the model bias-free. It does make the workflow more robust than a
single unpruned tree in many practical prediction settings.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students why averaging many overfit trees can still improve test performance if
the trees are not too strongly correlated.
:::

## Out-of-bag error is useful, but not an excuse to skip validation

Because each tree is fit on a bootstrap sample, some observations are left out of
that tree's training set. Those left-out observations can be used to form an
out-of-bag estimate of predictive performance.

That is convenient, but in this course it should still be treated as part of a wider
model-assessment workflow. Students should compare out-of-bag diagnostics with
cross-validation or a held-out test rule when the modelling decision matters.

## Where random forests fit in `TEM 0052`

Random forests extend the decision-tree block into ensemble methods. They help
students see a recurring pattern in machine learning:

- start with an interpretable baseline,
- identify the source of instability,
- and use resampling plus controlled randomness to improve prediction.

They are also a useful bridge to later topics such as gradient boosting, where the
ensemble idea remains but the optimization logic changes.

## What to report

When students present a random-forest workflow in `TEM 0052`, they should usually
report:

- the prediction task and the evaluation rule,
- the key tuning controls such as `n_estimators`, `max_depth`, or `max_features`,
- whether out-of-bag error was used and how it compared with cross-validation,
- whether the forest improved meaningfully on a single-tree or logistic baseline,
- and one short explanation of the cost of that gain in interpretability and runtime.
